meta.title: DBX-DEP-1: Advanced Data Processing and Modeling

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to process a stream of events from Kafka and write the results to a Delta table. The pipeline must guarantee exactly-once processing semantics. Which combination of Structured Streaming features achieves this?

options.text: Use `foreachBatch` with Delta table ACID transactions and checkpoint location
Use `outputMode('complete')` with a WAL (write-ahead log) enabled
Use `trigger(once=True)` with idempotent writes to S3
Use `foreachBatch` with Kafka offset tracking only, relying on at-least-once delivery

questions.explanation: Delta Lake's ACID transactions combined with Structured Streaming checkpoints (which track source offsets) provide exactly-once end-to-end semantics. `foreachBatch` allows custom write logic while the checkpoint ensures offsets are committed only after successful Delta commits. Option B is incorrect — `complete` mode rewrites the full result set and doesn't guarantee exactly-once on the source. Option C uses trigger once but doesn't address Kafka offset tracking within a stream. Option D only achieves at-least-once without the transactional Delta writes.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming + Delta Lake exactly-once guarantees

questions.stem: A Delta table has accumulated 500 small files over several days of streaming ingestion. A data engineer runs `OPTIMIZE` on the table. What is the PRIMARY effect of this operation?

options.text: Compacts small files into larger files and rewrites the transaction log to remove old entries
Compacts small files into larger files and updates statistics used by the query optimizer
Deletes all files older than the retention period and creates new compacted files
Rewrites the transaction log only, without touching Parquet data files

questions.explanation: OPTIMIZE compacts small Parquet files into larger ones (target ~1GB) and collects column statistics used by the Delta query optimizer for data skipping. It does NOT remove old files (that's VACUUM), does NOT rewrite the transaction log entries (the log still references old files until VACUUM runs), and does NOT touch the retention policy. The statistics update is critical for Z-ORDER effectiveness.
questions.reference: Delta Lake OPTIMIZE command

questions.stem: A pipeline reads from a Bronze Delta table and applies a `MERGE INTO` operation to upsert into a Silver table every 5 minutes. After several weeks, query performance on the Silver table degrades significantly. The table has 10 million rows and the merge key is `customer_id`. Which set of actions BEST addresses the performance issue?

options.text: Run `VACUUM` to remove old Parquet files, then add a Bloom filter index on `customer_id`
Run `OPTIMIZE ZORDER BY (customer_id)` and schedule regular `VACUUM` runs
Convert the merge to an `INSERT OVERWRITE` partitioned by `customer_id` hash buckets
Increase the shuffle partitions to 400 and add `customer_id` as a partition column

questions.explanation: After weeks of 5-minute MERGEs, the Silver table will have many small files and unordered data. OPTIMIZE ZORDER BY (customer_id) solves both problems: it compacts files AND co-locates rows with similar customer_id values so the MERGE can skip irrelevant files via data skipping. Regular VACUUM removes the now-obsolete old files to prevent the transaction log from growing. Option A (Bloom filters) helps point lookups but ZORDER is more effective for MERGE patterns. Option C (INSERT OVERWRITE) loses the upsert semantics. Option D (partitioning by customer_id) on a high-cardinality column would create too many small partitions.
questions.reference: Delta Lake performance tuning, ZORDER, MERGE optimization

questions.stem: An organization uses Unity Catalog. A data engineer needs to grant a team of analysts read access to a specific table in a schema, but NOT to other tables in the same schema. Which Unity Catalog privilege grants the minimum necessary permissions?

options.text: GRANT USE CATALOG, USE SCHEMA, SELECT ON TABLE analysts_team
GRANT USE CATALOG, USE SCHEMA, SELECT ON SCHEMA TO analysts_team
GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA TO analysts_team
GRANT READ ON TABLE TO analysts_team

questions.explanation: Unity Catalog uses a hierarchical privilege model. To read a specific table, a principal needs: USE CATALOG (to navigate into the catalog), USE SCHEMA (to navigate into the schema), and SELECT ON the specific TABLE. Option B grants SELECT on the entire SCHEMA which would expose all tables. Option C omits USE CATALOG and USE SCHEMA, which are required to traverse the hierarchy. Option D uses READ which is not a valid Unity Catalog privilege; SELECT is the correct privilege.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog privilege hierarchy

questions.stem: A Structured Streaming job uses `trigger(processingTime='5 minutes')`. The job fails and restarts after 12 minutes of downtime. What happens when the job restarts, assuming the checkpoint is intact?

options.text: The job processes only the data that arrived in the last 5 minutes, dropping the backlog
The job reads the checkpoint to determine the last committed offsets and processes all data since those offsets, catching up before returning to the 5-minute trigger cadence
The job fails to restart because the Kafka offsets have expired
The job replays all data from the beginning of the stream regardless of checkpoint state

questions.explanation: Structured Streaming checkpoints store the exact source offsets (e.g., Kafka topic/partition/offset) of the last successfully committed micro-batch. On restart, the engine reads the checkpoint to find where processing stopped, then reads ALL data since those offsets — catching up the 12-minute backlog before resuming the 5-minute trigger cadence. The trigger interval controls batch cadence during normal operation, not recovery behavior. Option A is wrong — no data is dropped if the checkpoint exists. Option C depends on Kafka retention settings, not the trigger. Option D only happens when no checkpoint exists.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming checkpointing and recovery

questions.stem: Which statement CORRECTLY describes the behavior of Delta Lake's Change Data Feed (CDF) when enabled on a table?

options.text: CDF captures INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations and stores them as additional rows with `_change_type`, `_commit_version`, and `_commit_timestamp` columns
CDF replaces the transaction log and stores a separate binary log of all DML operations
CDF only captures rows that are changed by UPDATE or DELETE, not new INSERTs
CDF is automatically enabled on all Delta tables in Unity Catalog and cannot be disabled

questions.explanation: When CDF is enabled (`TBLPROPERTIES (delta.enableChangeDataFeed = true)`), Delta Lake writes change data files alongside regular data files. These capture all row-level changes (insert, update_preimage, update_postimage, delete) with metadata columns: `_change_type`, `_commit_version`, and `_commit_timestamp`. You can read CDF data using `table_changes('table_name', startVersion)`. Option B is wrong — CDF doesn't replace the transaction log. Option C is wrong — INSERTs are captured. Option D is wrong — CDF must be explicitly enabled.
questions.reference: Delta Lake Change Data Feed

questions.stem: A Spark job is running slowly. Examining the Spark UI, a data engineer notices one task in a stage is taking 10x longer than all other tasks. The data is partitioned by `date`. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?

options.text: The cluster does not have enough executors
Data skew — one date partition contains significantly more data than others
The shuffle partition count is too high, causing excessive network overhead
The Spark driver is running out of memory

questions.explanation: When one task takes 10x longer than peers in the same stage, this is the classic signature of data skew. One partition (e.g., a single date with far more records — perhaps a high-traffic day) contains disproportionately more data than others, causing one executor to process much more work. Solutions include salting join keys, using AQE (Adaptive Query Execution) skew hints, or repartitioning. Option A (insufficient executors) would slow ALL tasks uniformly. Option C (too many shuffle partitions) causes many tiny tasks, not one massive one. Option D (driver OOM) would cause failures, not a slow single task.
questions.reference: Spark data skew, Adaptive Query Execution

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to query the state of a Delta table as it existed 48 hours ago using Time Travel. The table's `delta.logRetentionDuration` is set to `interval 7 days` and `delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration` is set to `interval 1 day`. VACUUM has been run recently. Can the query succeed?

options.text: Yes — the transaction log retains 7 days of history, so the query will succeed
No — VACUUM deleted files needed for the 48-hour snapshot because deletedFileRetentionDuration is only 1 day
Yes — Time Travel ignores VACUUM and always reads from the transaction log alone
No — Time Travel only supports version-based queries, not timestamp-based queries

questions.explanation: Time Travel requires BOTH the transaction log entries AND the underlying Parquet data files for the requested version. The transaction log retains 7 days of history (sufficient for 48 hours), BUT `deletedFileRetentionDuration = 1 day` means VACUUM removed the Parquet files that were marked as deleted/replaced more than 1 day ago. Since those files are physically gone, the 48-hour-ago query will fail. The log entry exists but points to missing files. To support 48-hour Time Travel, `deletedFileRetentionDuration` must be >= 48 hours (2 days). Option C is wrong — the log alone is insufficient without the data files.
questions.reference: Delta Lake Time Travel, VACUUM retention settings

questions.stem: A data engineer is designing a medallion architecture pipeline. Which TWO statements CORRECTLY describe best practices for the Silver layer?

options.text: Apply data quality checks and reject or quarantine records that fail validation
Store raw, unmodified data exactly as received from source systems
Join and enrich data from multiple Bronze sources to create a conformed, cleansed dataset
Apply complex business aggregations and KPI calculations to produce reporting-ready datasets
Enforce schema consistency and parse/cast data types appropriately

questions.explanation: The Silver layer is the cleansed, validated, conformed layer. Best practices: (A) Apply data quality checks — Silver should contain only valid, trusted data; records failing checks go to a quarantine table. (E) Enforce schema and types — Bronze may have strings for everything; Silver applies proper types and schema. Option B describes Bronze (raw, unmodified). Option C (joining multiple sources) is sometimes done in Silver for entity resolution but is more characteristic of Gold. Option D (aggregations, KPIs) describes Gold layer.
questions.reference: Medallion architecture — Silver layer responsibilities

questions.stem: In Unity Catalog, what is the purpose of a Storage Credential?

options.text: It stores username/password pairs for external database connections
It encapsulates a cloud IAM role or service principal that Unity Catalog uses to access external cloud storage on behalf of users
It defines which users can access data in the Unity Catalog metastore
It encrypts data at rest in Delta tables using customer-managed keys

questions.explanation: A Storage Credential in Unity Catalog is an abstraction over a cloud IAM role (AWS) or service principal (Azure) or service account (GCP). It represents the cloud-level credentials that Databricks uses to access external cloud storage locations (S3, ADLS, GCS). Users don't interact with the underlying cloud credentials directly — Unity Catalog manages access through the storage credential and external location objects. Option A describes JDBC connection secrets. Option C describes Unity Catalog privileges/ACLs. Option D describes encryption, which is separate.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog Storage Credentials and External Locations

questions.stem:
A data engineer writes the following Structured Streaming query:

```python
df = spark.readStream.format('delta').table('events')
result = df.groupBy('user_id', window('timestamp', '1 hour')).count()
result.writeStream.format('delta').outputMode('append').table('hourly_counts')
```

What will happen when this query runs?

options.text: The query runs successfully and appends new hourly window counts to `hourly_counts`
The query fails — aggregations with `window()` require `outputMode('complete')` or `outputMode('update')`
The query runs but produces incorrect results because `append` mode doesn't support late data
The query fails because Delta tables cannot be used as a source for aggregation queries

questions.explanation: Streaming aggregations with time windows require either `complete` mode (recompute and output the full result set each batch) or `update` mode (output only rows that changed). `append` mode is only valid for aggregations when watermarking is configured AND the window has closed (no more late data possible). Without a watermark, Spark cannot determine when a window is finalized and thus cannot safely use append mode. The query will throw an AnalysisException at runtime. Option A is incorrect for the reason above. Option C is partially right about late data but the actual failure is the mode mismatch. Option D is wrong — Delta tables are fully supported as streaming sources.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming output modes, windowed aggregations

questions.stem: A Delta Live Tables (DLT) pipeline is configured with `development` mode. What is a key behavioral difference compared to `production` mode?

options.text: Development mode skips all data quality constraint checks to speed up iteration
Development mode reuses the cluster between pipeline runs to reduce startup time and allows interactive troubleshooting
Development mode writes data to a separate staging catalog automatically
Development mode processes only the first 100 rows of each source table

questions.explanation: In DLT development mode, the pipeline cluster is kept alive between runs (not terminated after each run), which dramatically reduces iteration time since cluster startup is avoided. Developers can also attach notebooks to the running cluster for interactive debugging. Production mode terminates the cluster after each run for cost efficiency and treats failures as permanent (triggering alerts). Option A is wrong — constraints are enforced in both modes. Option C is wrong — development mode writes to the same target, not a staging catalog. Option D is wrong — there is no automatic row limit.
questions.reference: Delta Live Tables development vs production mode

questions.stem: A table contains `customer_id`, `address`, `start_date`, and `end_date` columns to implement Slowly Changing Dimension Type 2 (SCD2). A new address arrives for a customer who already has an open record (`end_date IS NULL`). Which SQL logic CORRECTLY implements the SCD2 update?

options.text: UPDATE the existing row to set the new address and update `start_date` to today
INSERT a new row with the new address; the old row remains unchanged
UPDATE the existing open row to set `end_date` to today, then INSERT a new row with the new address and `start_date` = today, `end_date` = NULL
DELETE the existing row and INSERT a new row with the new address

questions.explanation: SCD Type 2 tracks the full history of changes by maintaining multiple rows per entity. The correct process: (1) Close the current open record by setting its `end_date` to the effective date of the change, (2) Insert a new row with the updated value, a new `start_date`, and `end_date = NULL` (indicating it's the current record). This preserves the historical record with its valid time range. Option A (in-place update) is SCD Type 1 — loses history. Option B (insert only, no close) creates two 'open' records for the same customer. Option D (delete + insert) destroys history.
questions.reference: SCD Type 2 implementation with Delta Lake MERGE

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the `_delta_log` directory in a Delta table?

options.text: It stores compressed backups of all Parquet data files for point-in-time recovery
It contains JSON and Parquet checkpoint files that record all transactions, schema changes, and file additions/removals — forming the transaction log
It stores Spark application logs and metrics for all operations on the table
It is a temporary directory used during OPTIMIZE operations and is cleaned up after completion

questions.explanation: The `_delta_log` directory IS the Delta transaction log — it's what makes a directory of Parquet files a Delta table. It contains: numbered JSON files (00000000000000000000.json, etc.) recording each transaction (AddFile, RemoveFile, CommitInfo, MetaData actions), and Parquet checkpoint files every 10 commits that consolidate the log for faster reading. Reading the log allows reconstruction of the exact state of the table at any version (enabling Time Travel). The log does NOT store backups (that's the actual data files), Spark logs, or temp files.
questions.reference: Delta Lake transaction log internals

questions.stem: A data engineer uses Auto Loader to ingest files from cloud storage into a Bronze Delta table. What mechanism does Auto Loader use by DEFAULT to efficiently track which files have been processed?

options.text: It lists all files in the directory on each run and compares them to a watermark stored in the checkpoint
It uses cloud storage event notifications (e.g., AWS S3 Event Notifications or Azure Event Grid) to detect new files without directory listing
It uses a file notification service backed by a message queue, triggered by the cloud provider's object storage events
It monitors file modification timestamps and processes any file modified since the last checkpoint

questions.explanation: Auto Loader's DEFAULT mode is directory listing mode — it lists the source directory on each trigger and uses the checkpoint to track which files have already been processed (comparing against a persisted file state store). This works without any cloud infrastructure setup. Auto Loader also supports a 'file notification' mode (Option C / similar to B) that uses cloud event notifications (SNS+SQS on AWS, Event Grid + Queue Storage on Azure) for higher scale without repeated directory listing — but this requires additional setup and is NOT the default. Option D (modification timestamps) is unreliable and not how Auto Loader works.
questions.reference: Auto Loader file discovery modes

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to implement row-level security in Unity Catalog so that users can only see rows from a table where `region` matches their assigned region (stored in a separate `user_regions` table). What is the CORRECT approach?

options.text: Create a view that joins the target table with `user_regions` filtering by `current_user()`, and grant SELECT on the view instead of the table
Use `GRANT SELECT ON TABLE WHERE region = current_user()` syntax
Enable row-level security in the table's TBLPROPERTIES with a filter expression
Use a Delta table with a partition on `region` and grant partition-level access per user

questions.explanation: Unity Catalog implements row-level security through secure views. Create a view that filters the table using `current_user()` (or `is_member()` for group-based) joined against an access control table, then revoke direct table access and grant SELECT only on the view. The view owner's permissions are used for the underlying table access, while the filter ensures each user sees only their permitted rows. Option B is invalid SQL syntax — Unity Catalog doesn't support conditional WHERE clauses in GRANT statements. Option C doesn't exist as a TBLPROPERTIES feature. Option D (partition-level grants) is not a supported Unity Catalog feature.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog row-level security with views

questions.stem: In Databricks, what does the term 'spillage' refer to in the context of Spark execution?

options.text: Data written to the driver node when executor memory is exhausted
Intermediate shuffle data written to disk when it exceeds available executor memory
Delta table data written to a backup location during OPTIMIZE
Network data dropped when the shuffle block size exceeds the transfer limit

questions.explanation: Spillage (or disk spill) occurs when Spark's in-memory data structures (shuffle buffers, sort buffers, aggregation maps) exceed available executor memory. Spark writes the overflow data to local disk storage, then reads it back during subsequent processing stages. This drastically degrades performance due to disk I/O. Spillage is visible in the Spark UI under the 'Shuffle Spill (Memory)' and 'Shuffle Spill (Disk)' metrics in stage details. Solutions: increase executor memory, reduce data per partition, or optimize joins. Option A is wrong — data going to driver is a 'collect' operation issue. Options C and D don't describe spillage.
questions.reference: Spark memory management, shuffle spill

options.text: The pipeline fails and stops processing when any record violates the constraint
Violating records are dropped from the output table; violation counts are tracked in the pipeline event log
Violating records are written to a quarantine table named `transactions_quarantine`
Violating records are kept in the table with a `_quality_flag` column set to 'invalid'

questions.explanation: `@dlt.expect_or_drop` silently drops rows that fail the constraint and continues pipeline execution. The violations are NOT quarantined automatically (that requires a separate quarantine table pattern). The violation statistics (records dropped, constraint name) are captured in the DLT event log and visible in the pipeline UI under data quality metrics. Compare with: `@dlt.expect` (keep bad rows, record violations), `@dlt.expect_or_fail` (halt pipeline on any violation), `@dlt.expect_all_or_drop` (multi-constraint drop). Option C is wrong — DLT does not automatically create quarantine tables. Options A and D describe different expectation decorators.
questions.reference: Delta Live Tables data quality expectations

questions.stem: Which statement BEST describes the difference between `DEEP CLONE` and `SHALLOW CLONE` in Delta Lake?

options.text: DEEP CLONE copies both data files and the transaction log; SHALLOW CLONE copies only the transaction log and references the original data files
DEEP CLONE copies only the schema; SHALLOW CLONE copies schema and data
DEEP CLONE is incremental; SHALLOW CLONE always performs a full copy
DEEP CLONE supports Time Travel; SHALLOW CLONE does not

questions.explanation: DEEP CLONE physically copies all Parquet data files and the transaction log to the destination — creating a fully independent copy. Changes to the original don't affect the clone. SHALLOW CLONE only copies the transaction log (and table metadata) but references the source table's data files in place. It's fast and low-cost but: (1) if the source files are deleted (e.g., by VACUUM), the shallow clone breaks, (2) the shallow clone cannot be used if the source becomes unavailable. Use DEEP CLONE for independent backups; SHALLOW CLONE for lightweight testing/development with the understanding of the dependency.
questions.reference: Delta Lake CLONE operations

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to read only the most recent record per `device_id` from a large Delta table with 500 million rows. The table is NOT partitioned. What query approach provides the BEST performance?

options.text: Use `SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY device_id ORDER BY event_time DESC) as rn FROM table WHERE rn = 1`
Run `OPTIMIZE ZORDER BY (device_id, event_time)` first, then query with `SELECT * FROM table QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY device_id ORDER BY event_time DESC) = 1`
Use `SELECT * FROM table GROUP BY device_id HAVING MAX(event_time)`
Filter with `WHERE event_time = (SELECT MAX(event_time) FROM table)` to get the single most recent timestamp

questions.explanation: Option B is best for two reasons: (1) OPTIMIZE ZORDER BY (device_id, event_time) co-locates data for each device_id, allowing Delta's data skipping to skip entire files when filtering/partitioning by device_id during the window function — massively reducing I/O on 500M rows. (2) `QUALIFY` is the correct, clean SQL syntax for filtering window function results without a subquery (supported in Databricks SQL / Spark SQL). Option A is incorrect SQL — you can't reference a window function alias in WHERE without a subquery. Option C is invalid SQL — HAVING with MAX(event_time) alone doesn't return the full row. Option D returns all rows at the single global maximum timestamp, not one row per device.
questions.reference: Delta Lake ZORDER, QUALIFY clause, window functions

questions.stem:
A data engineer configures a watermark in a Structured Streaming query:

```python
df.withWatermark('event_time', '10 minutes')
  .groupBy(window('event_time', '5 minutes'), 'device_id')
  .count()
  .writeStream.outputMode('append')
```

Data arrives with event times at 12:00, 12:03, 12:07, and then a late record arrives at 12:02 when the watermark has advanced to 12:05. What happens to the late record?

options.text: The late record is processed and updates the 12:00-12:05 window
The late record is dropped because its event time (12:02) is before the watermark (12:05)
The late record triggers a recomputation of all previous windows
The late record is stored in a dead-letter queue for manual review

questions.explanation: With a 10-minute watermark, Spark considers data late if it arrives more than 10 minutes after the max seen event time. When the watermark is at 12:05, the engine has committed the 12:00-12:05 window in `append` mode and will not update it. The late record at 12:02 falls in the 12:00-12:05 window, which is past the watermark threshold — so it is silently dropped. In `append` mode, windows are only emitted once (when the watermark passes the window end + watermark delay), and they cannot be updated afterward. Option A is wrong — the window is closed. Option C doesn't happen. Option D is wrong — Spark doesn't have a built-in dead-letter queue for late records.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming watermarking, late data handling

questions.stem: Which TWO methods can be used to mask sensitive data in Unity Catalog to ensure analysts cannot see raw PII values while still being able to query the table?

options.text: Create a view that applies masking functions (e.g., `mask()`, `regexp_replace()`) to PII columns and grant access to the view instead of the table
Use Unity Catalog's built-in column masking policies to dynamically apply masking based on user group membership
Enable transparent data encryption (TDE) on the Delta table to encrypt PII columns
Partition the table by a non-PII column and revoke access to PII partitions
Use Delta table column-level encryption with `ENCRYPT()` functions in a DLT pipeline

questions.explanation: Two valid PII masking approaches in Unity Catalog: (A) Secure views with masking functions — create a view applying SHA256, MASK(), or regexp_replace() to PII columns, grant SELECT on the view only. Classic, widely-used pattern. (B) Unity Catalog column masking policies (available in Databricks' newer Unity Catalog versions) — attach a masking function as a policy on a column; the policy applies dynamically based on user identity/group without requiring a separate view. Option C (TDE) encrypts at rest but doesn't mask query results — analysts would still see plaintext. Option D (partition revocation) doesn't protect columns within accessible partitions. Option E describes a non-standard approach not available in standard DLT/Delta.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog column masking, secure views for PII

questions.stem: What is the PRIMARY advantage of using `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` adaptive setting in Databricks (Adaptive Query Execution - AQE) over a fixed partition count?

options.text: AQE eliminates the need for broadcast joins by automatically upgrading all joins to sort-merge joins
AQE dynamically coalesces shuffle partitions based on actual data size after each shuffle, reducing overhead from many small partitions
AQE pre-partitions source data before reading to avoid shuffle entirely
AQE sets shuffle partitions to 1 to minimize network transfer

questions.explanation: AQE's dynamic partition coalescing addresses a core Spark tuning challenge: you can't know the right shuffle partition count before the job runs. AQE collects statistics after each shuffle stage and automatically coalesces many small partitions into fewer, right-sized partitions for the next stage. This avoids both the overhead of thousands of tiny tasks (high scheduling overhead) and the memory pressure of too few large partitions. AQE also handles skew join optimization and dynamic broadcast join switching. Option A is wrong — AQE promotes small relations to broadcast joins (opposite direction). Options C and D are incorrect.
questions.reference: Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) in Spark/Databricks

questions.stem: A DLT pipeline is failing intermittently with records being quarantined due to a data quality constraint. The data engineer wants to view historical trends of constraint violations over time. Where can this information be found?

options.text: The Spark UI history server under the 'SQL' tab
The DLT pipeline event log, queryable as a Delta table at the pipeline's storage location
The cluster driver logs accessible via `dbutils.fs.ls('/databricks/driver/')`
The Unity Catalog audit log in the account console

questions.explanation: DLT maintains an event log as a Delta table stored in the pipeline's configured storage location (typically `<storage_location>/system/events`). This event log captures: pipeline run history, data quality constraint violation counts per batch, flow expectations results, data metrics (rows processed, rows dropped), and error details. You can query it with standard SQL: `SELECT * FROM event_log('<pipeline_id>') WHERE event_type = 'flow_progress'`. This enables trend analysis, alerting, and audit of data quality over time. Option A (Spark UI) doesn't retain DLT-specific data quality metrics. Option C (driver logs) has text logs, not structured metrics. Option D (Unity Catalog audit log) tracks access events, not DLT metrics.
questions.reference: DLT pipeline event log, data quality monitoring

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to implement a pipeline that processes CDC (Change Data Capture) events from a source system. Events arrive as INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations with a sequence number. Which approach CORRECTLY handles all three operation types when writing to a Delta Silver table?

options.text: Use `APPLY CHANGES INTO` in DLT (or `MERGE INTO` in Spark) matching on the primary key, applying updates/inserts, and deleting rows marked as DELETE
Use `INSERT INTO ... SELECT` for all event types and rely on downstream deduplication
Use `INSERT OVERWRITE` to replace the entire table with the latest state on each run
Use `COPY INTO` which natively handles CDC semantics including deletes

questions.explanation: CDC processing requires handling all three operation types correctly. `APPLY CHANGES INTO` (DLT's native CDC feature) is specifically designed for this: it processes ordered CDC events using a sequence column, handles INSERT/UPDATE as upserts (MERGE), and DELETE events as row removals. In standard Spark, a `MERGE INTO` with three WHEN clauses handles this: WHEN MATCHED AND op='DELETE' THEN DELETE, WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE, WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT. Option B (INSERT only) can't handle deletes or deduplication properly. Option C (INSERT OVERWRITE) rewrites the entire table per batch — unscalable for large tables and doesn't handle deletes incrementally. Option D (COPY INTO) is for bulk loading files, not CDC event processing.
questions.reference: DLT APPLY CHANGES INTO, MERGE INTO for CDC

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates a Delta table with the following DDL:

```sql
CREATE TABLE sales (
  sale_id BIGINT,
  sale_date DATE,
  amount DECIMAL(10,2)
) PARTITIONED BY (sale_date)
LOCATION 's3://bucket/sales';
```

After 2 years, the table has 730 partitions. What is the MOST LIKELY performance issue with this design?

options.text: Queries filtering by `sale_date` will be slower because partitioning disables data skipping
The 730 partitions create excessive metadata overhead in the transaction log and too many small files if daily data volumes are low
Delta Lake does not support DATE columns as partition keys
The S3 path structure will exceed the maximum path length limit

questions.explanation: Partitioning by `sale_date` (date granularity) can be problematic in two ways: (1) If each day has modest data volume (e.g., <128MB), each partition contains very small files — Delta's data skipping is more efficient on fewer larger files. (2) 730 partitions means 730 directory listings and potentially thousands of file entries in the transaction log, adding metadata overhead. The Databricks recommendation is to avoid high-cardinality partitioning when partition sizes would be < ~1GB; instead use ZORDER and rely on data skipping. Exception: if daily data is consistently large (>1GB per day), date partitioning works well. Option A is wrong — partitioning works with data skipping. Option C is wrong — DATE is a valid partition type. Option D is wrong.
questions.reference: Delta Lake partitioning best practices

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of the Databricks `dbutils.secrets.get()` function?

options.text: To encrypt sensitive configuration values before storing them in Delta tables
To retrieve secret values (API keys, passwords, tokens) from a secret scope without exposing them in notebook output or logs
To validate that a secret meets organizational password complexity requirements
To share secrets between workspaces in a Unity Catalog deployment

questions.explanation: `dbutils.secrets.get(scope, key)` retrieves a secret value from a Databricks secret scope (backed by Databricks or Azure Key Vault). The key security feature: the returned value is a string that Databricks redacts `[REDACTED]` if it appears in notebook cell output or Spark logs, preventing accidental exposure. This enables safely passing credentials to JDBC connections, API calls, etc. without hardcoding. Option A is wrong — it retrieves, doesn't encrypt. Option C doesn't exist. Option D is wrong — secrets are workspace-scoped (though Unity Catalog has its own cross-workspace capabilities).
questions.reference: Databricks Secret Management, dbutils.secrets

questions.stem: A Spark job processes 1TB of data in a shuffle-heavy join operation. The job completes but takes 45 minutes. The data engineer notices in the Spark UI that Stage 3 has 200 tasks, and 195 of them complete in under 30 seconds while 5 tasks each take over 10 minutes. Which optimization is MOST LIKELY to help?

options.text: Increase `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` from 200 to 2000 to distribute work more evenly
Enable AQE skew join optimization with `spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled=true` and ensure AQE is enabled
Add more worker nodes to provide additional executor memory
Use `BROADCAST` hint to broadcast the larger table in the join

questions.explanation: The symptom — 195 fast tasks and 5 extremely slow tasks in the same stage — is the classic data skew pattern. AQE's skew join optimization detects skewed partitions (where one partition is much larger than the median) and automatically splits them into smaller sub-partitions that multiple tasks can process in parallel. This is the targeted fix. Option A (more partitions) won't help if the skew is inherent in the data — you'd still have 5 huge partitions among 2000. Option C (more nodes) doesn't fix skew — the 5 slow tasks are limited by data distribution, not compute. Option D (broadcast) is for small tables; 1TB join tables are too large to broadcast.
questions.reference: AQE skew join optimization, data skew handling

questions.stem: A data engineer writes a PySpark DataFrame transformation that adds a new column using a Python UDF. A colleague suggests replacing it with a built-in Spark SQL function. Why would the built-in function GENERALLY outperform the Python UDF?

options.text: Python UDFs run in the JVM, while built-in functions use a Python interpreter — built-ins avoid Python startup overhead
Python UDFs require row-by-row serialization between the JVM and Python processes, while built-in functions execute natively in the JVM with Tungsten optimizations
Python UDFs cannot process null values, while built-in functions handle nulls automatically
Python UDFs are deprecated in Databricks Runtime 12+ and will throw an error

questions.explanation: Python UDFs impose a significant performance penalty due to serialization overhead: each row (or batch in Pandas UDF mode) must be serialized from the JVM (where Spark runs) to a Python process, processed, then serialized back. This 'pickle-and-transfer' pattern is 10-100x slower than native operations. Spark's built-in functions execute directly in the JVM using Tungsten's code generation and optimized binary processing — no inter-process communication. The fix for UDF performance is to: (1) use built-in functions when possible, (2) use Pandas UDFs (vectorized) which transfer Arrow batches instead of row-by-row. Option A has it backwards — UDFs run in Python, built-ins in JVM. Option C is wrong — UDFs can handle nulls. Option D is wrong.
questions.reference: Spark Python UDF performance, Tungsten execution engine

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to enforce schema evolution on a Delta table so that new columns added to incoming data are automatically added to the table schema. Which configuration enables this?

options.text: Set `spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled = true` before running the write, or use `.option('mergeSchema', 'true')` in the write command
Use `CONVERT TO DELTA` with the `--schema-evolution` flag
Set `spark.databricks.delta.schema.strict = false` in the cluster configuration
Use `writeStream` with `outputMode('append')` which automatically merges schemas

questions.explanation: Delta schema evolution (adding new columns from incoming data) is controlled by: (1) `.option('mergeSchema', 'true')` in the DataFrameWriter — applies to a single write operation, OR (2) the session config `spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled = true` — applies to all Delta writes in the session. With merge schema enabled, if the incoming DataFrame has columns not in the table schema, those columns are added to the table. This is different from schema overwrite (`overwriteSchema`), which replaces the schema. Option B doesn't exist with that syntax. Option C is not a valid config. Option D is incorrect — append mode does not automatically merge schemas.
questions.reference: Delta Lake schema evolution, mergeSchema option

questions.stem: A data engineer uses `foreachBatch` in a Structured Streaming job to write to a non-Delta external database (e.g., PostgreSQL). The database does not support ACID transactions. Which technique makes the write IDEMPOTENT so that re-processing the same micro-batch does not produce duplicate rows?

options.text: Use a DELETE then INSERT pattern keyed on the batch ID within the `foreachBatch` function, and always commit the batch ID atomically with the data
Enable `outputMode('complete')` to overwrite the entire target table on each micro-batch
Store the checkpoint in a Delta table and compare incoming rows against it before writing
Set `spark.streaming.maxOffsetsPerTrigger` to 1 so each micro-batch contains a single row

questions.explanation: For exactly-once semantics with non-transactional sinks, the `foreachBatch` function should implement idempotent writes. A common pattern: (1) persist a unique batch ID alongside the data, (2) before each write, delete any rows from a previous run of the same batch ID, (3) insert the new rows. If the batch is replayed (e.g., after a failure), the delete removes the previously written rows and the insert re-writes them — net result is idempotent. This is the 'upsert by batch ID' pattern. Option B (complete mode) rewrites the entire table, which may not be viable for large external databases and doesn't generalize. Option C is not a valid pattern — the checkpoint tracks source offsets, not output rows. Option D reduces throughput to a crawl and doesn't guarantee idempotency.
questions.reference: foreachBatch idempotent writes to external sinks, exactly-once semantics

questions.stem: A data engineer builds a multi-hop streaming pipeline where Bronze, Silver, and Gold tables are all populated by Structured Streaming jobs. The Gold stream reads from the Silver Delta table as a streaming source. When a record is corrected in Bronze and propagates to Silver via an UPDATE, what happens in the Gold streaming job reading Silver?

options.text: The Gold stream automatically picks up the corrected row because Delta streaming sources support UPDATE events natively
The Gold stream will NOT see the correction unless Change Data Feed is enabled on Silver and the Gold stream uses CDF as its source
Delta streaming sources only emit rows appended to the table; UPDATEs to Silver rows are invisible to the Gold stream by default
The Gold streaming job will fail with an error when it detects the Silver table was modified by a non-append operation

questions.explanation: By default, reading a Delta table as a streaming source (`spark.readStream.table(...)`) operates in append-only mode — the stream sees only newly appended rows. UPDATE and DELETE operations on the Silver table do NOT surface as changed rows to the Gold streaming job. This is the fundamental limitation of multi-hop streaming pipelines that include non-append operations. To propagate updates downstream, the Gold stream must use Delta Change Data Feed: enable CDF on Silver (`delta.enableChangeDataFeed = true`), then read from Silver using `.option('readChangeFeed', 'true')`, which exposes update_preimage, update_postimage, insert, and delete events. Option A is wrong — standard Delta streaming is append-only. Option B is partially right about CDF being the solution but misstates when the issue occurs. Option D is wrong — no error is thrown; updates are simply invisible.
questions.reference: Delta streaming source: append-only behavior, CDF for multi-hop update propagation

questions.stem: A data engineer has a Structured Streaming job that uses `trigger(once=True)` to run as a batch job on a schedule. A colleague suggests switching to `trigger(availableNow=True)`. What is the KEY operational difference?

options.text: `trigger(availableNow=True)` processes data in multiple micro-batches (exploiting parallelism within the available data), while `trigger(once=True)` processes all available data in a single large micro-batch
`trigger(availableNow=True)` is deprecated and will be removed in a future Databricks Runtime
`trigger(once=True)` uses checkpoints; `trigger(availableNow=True)` does not use checkpoints
`trigger(availableNow=True)` keeps the stream alive indefinitely, waiting for new data

questions.explanation: `trigger(once=True)` processes all available data in ONE micro-batch, no matter how large. If there is 100GB of new data, it is processed as a single mega-batch, which can cause memory pressure and long run times. `trigger(availableNow=True)` was introduced to fix this: it processes available data across MULTIPLE micro-batches (up to `maxFilesPerTrigger` or similar rate limits per batch), then terminates when all available data is consumed. Both modes: (a) use checkpoints, (b) terminate after processing, (c) maintain exactly-once guarantees. The difference is throughput and stability for large backlogs. Option B is wrong — availableNow is the modern recommended approach. Option C is wrong — both use checkpoints. Option D is wrong — availableNow terminates after consuming available data.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming: trigger(once) vs trigger(availableNow) behavior

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to implement custom stateful logic in Structured Streaming to track the state of each user session (open/closed) and emit alerts when a session is idle for more than 30 minutes. Which Structured Streaming API is MOST appropriate?

options.text: `groupBy().count()` with a 30-minute window and watermark
`flatMapGroupsWithState` with a custom `GroupState` update function and timeout set to `GroupStateTimeout.ProcessingTimeTimeout`
`foreachBatch` writing intermediate state to an external Redis store
`dropDuplicates('user_id', 'session_id')` with a 30-minute watermark

questions.explanation: `flatMapGroupsWithState` (and its sibling `mapGroupsWithState`) provides arbitrary stateful operations in Structured Streaming. For each key (e.g., `user_id`), you define: (1) the state schema (session open/closed, last activity timestamp), (2) the update function applied to each new event batch for that key, (3) timeout behavior — `GroupStateTimeout.ProcessingTimeTimeout` fires the update function with no new data after the specified processing-time timeout, enabling idle session detection. When the timeout fires, the function can emit an alert event. This is the canonical API for complex per-key stateful logic that window aggregations cannot express. Option A (window count) can detect activity but not arbitrary state machines. Option C (external Redis) works but bypasses Spark's built-in fault-tolerant state management. Option D (dropDuplicates) is for deduplication, not session tracking.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming flatMapGroupsWithState, custom stateful operations

questions.stem: A data engineer queries the DLT pipeline event log to find how many records were processed and how many were dropped by a specific expectation in the most recent pipeline run. Which table function provides access to this event log?

options.text: `SELECT * FROM delta.pipeline_events WHERE pipeline_id = '...'`
`SELECT * FROM event_log('<pipeline_id>') WHERE event_type = 'flow_progress'`
`SELECT * FROM system.runtime.pipeline_metrics WHERE pipeline_id = '...'`
`SELECT * FROM information_schema.pipeline_runs WHERE pipeline_id = '...'`

questions.explanation: The DLT event log is accessed using the `event_log('<pipeline_id>')` table-valued function. Events of type `'flow_progress'` contain the data quality metrics, including per-expectation counts of records that passed and failed validation. The result is a Delta table stored at `<pipeline_storage_location>/system/events` but is conveniently queried via the function without knowing the storage path. Option A uses an invalid table reference format. Option C references `system.runtime.pipeline_metrics`, which does not exist. Option D references `information_schema.pipeline_runs`, which is not a DLT-specific table in standard Unity Catalog INFORMATION_SCHEMA.
questions.reference: DLT event log: event_log() table function, flow_progress events

questions.stem: A data engineer configures a Kafka source in Structured Streaming with `startingOffsets` and `maxOffsetsPerTrigger`. Select TWO TRUE statements about these options.

options.text: `startingOffsets` is only used on the FIRST run of a new stream; subsequent runs use the checkpoint to determine starting offsets, ignoring `startingOffsets`
`startingOffsets` controls the offset position for every micro-batch, overriding the checkpoint on each restart
`maxOffsetsPerTrigger` limits the maximum number of Kafka messages processed per micro-batch, preventing the stream from being overwhelmed by a large backlog
`maxOffsetsPerTrigger` sets the minimum number of records required before a micro-batch fires
`startingOffsets` can be set to `'earliest'` to read all historical data from Kafka, or `'latest'` to read only new data

questions.explanation: Option A is correct and important: `startingOffsets` specifies where the stream should start reading on its FIRST run only (when no checkpoint exists). Once a checkpoint is established, subsequent restarts use the committed offsets from the checkpoint — `startingOffsets` is ignored. This is the correct behavior for fault tolerance. Option C is correct: `maxOffsetsPerTrigger` caps the number of Kafka messages consumed per micro-batch. This is essential for managing backlog during catch-up — without it, a large backlog would create one enormous micro-batch causing memory pressure. Option B is wrong — `startingOffsets` is only for the first run. Option D is wrong — `maxOffsetsPerTrigger` is a maximum, not a minimum threshold. Option E is true as a description of valid `startingOffsets` values, but the statement is correct only for the first run, which is covered more precisely in option A.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming Kafka source: startingOffsets, maxOffsetsPerTrigger

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to union two streaming DataFrames from different Kafka topics that have the same schema. Which code correctly creates a unified streaming DataFrame?

options.text: `df1.join(df2, on='event_id', how='full')` where both df1 and df2 are streaming
`df1.union(df2)` where both df1 and df2 are readStream DataFrames with the same schema
`spark.readStream.table('topic1').merge(spark.readStream.table('topic2'))`
`df1.append(df2)` where both df1 and df2 are streaming DataFrames

questions.explanation: `df1.union(df2)` is the supported way to combine two streaming DataFrames with the same schema. Structured Streaming supports `union()` on two or more streaming DataFrames — the unified stream emits records from either source as they arrive. Both sources are read in the same streaming query and their outputs are merged. This is useful for consuming multiple Kafka topics or S3 prefixes in a single pipeline. Option A (full join of two streams) is a stream-stream join — a different operation that requires matching keys and watermarks, not a simple union. Option C is not valid PySpark API. Option D (`append`) is not a DataFrame API method.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming: union of multiple streaming DataFrames

questions.stem: In a DLT pipeline using `APPLY CHANGES INTO`, a data engineer specifies `EXCEPT (audit_column)` in the statement. What does the `EXCEPT` clause do?

options.text: It filters out records where `audit_column` is NULL from CDC processing
It excludes `audit_column` from being used as a key for matching source and target rows
It prevents `audit_column` from being overwritten in the target table during UPDATE operations, preserving the original value
It drops `audit_column` from the target table's schema

questions.explanation: In `APPLY CHANGES INTO`, the `EXCEPT (column_list)` clause specifies columns that should NOT be updated in the target table when a matching row is found. This is commonly used for audit columns like `created_at`, `created_by`, or `record_version` — you want these to retain the values from when the row was first inserted, not be overwritten by subsequent updates from the source CDC stream. The EXCEPT columns are still part of the target table schema; they just aren't touched during UPDATE matching. Option A is wrong — EXCEPT does not filter rows based on NULL values. Option B is wrong — keys are specified with the `KEYS` clause, not EXCEPT. Option D is wrong — EXCEPT doesn't modify the schema.
questions.reference: DLT APPLY CHANGES INTO: EXCEPT clause for preserving target columns

questions.stem: A data engineer uses Auto Loader with `cloudFiles.schemaHints` to specify that a field called `event_value` should be treated as `DOUBLE` instead of the inferred `STRING`. The incoming JSON files sometimes have `event_value` as `'1.5'` (string-formatted). What is the effect of specifying `cloudFiles.schemaHints = 'event_value DOUBLE'`?

options.text: Auto Loader will fail with a schema mismatch error if any file contains `event_value` as a string
Auto Loader will cast `event_value` to DOUBLE during ingestion; values that cannot be cast will become NULL and may be captured in the rescued data column
`cloudFiles.schemaHints` overrides the entire inferred schema, replacing all other column types
`cloudFiles.schemaHints` only applies to schema inference during the first run; subsequent runs use the stored schema regardless of hints

questions.explanation: `cloudFiles.schemaHints` allows overriding the inferred type for specific columns without replacing the entire inferred schema. When `event_value DOUBLE` is specified, Auto Loader will attempt to cast the string `'1.5'` to DOUBLE during ingestion — which succeeds (1.5). If a value like `'abc'` cannot be cast to DOUBLE, it becomes NULL (and if `rescuedDataColumn` is configured, the original value is preserved there). This is a partial type override — only hinted columns change; all others remain auto-inferred. Option A is wrong — Auto Loader performs a best-effort cast, not strict validation. Option C is wrong — schema hints are partial overrides, not full replacements. Option D is wrong — schema hints apply on every run, but once stored in `schemaLocation`, the persisted schema is the effective schema.
questions.reference: Auto Loader cloudFiles.schemaHints: partial type overrides

questions.stem:
A data engineer needs to deduplicate streaming events from Kafka where the same `event_id` may arrive multiple times within a 1-hour window. They use:

```python
df.withWatermark('event_time', '1 hour')
  .dropDuplicates(['event_id', 'event_time'])
```

What is the purpose of including `event_time` alongside `event_id` in `dropDuplicates`?

options.text: Including `event_time` ensures that only records from the same second are deduplicated, allowing the same `event_id` in different seconds to be retained
The watermark on `event_time` bounds the state maintained for deduplication — without a watermarked time column in `dropDuplicates`, Spark would keep state forever, potentially causing OOM
Including `event_time` makes deduplication faster by reducing the number of state comparisons
`dropDuplicates` requires exactly two columns — one key and one time column — and will fail with only `event_id`

questions.explanation: Streaming deduplication with `dropDuplicates` must manage state — it needs to remember which `event_id` values have been seen to detect future duplicates. Without bounding this state, Spark keeps growing the state store indefinitely, eventually causing out-of-memory errors. By including the watermarked column (`event_time`) in `dropDuplicates`, the state is automatically purged for events older than the watermark (1 hour in this case). Only event IDs within the watermark window are retained in state — old state is evicted. Option A is wrong — including `event_time` as a dedup key would mean the same `event_id` at different times is NOT considered a duplicate, which is usually not the intent. The correct approach for true deduplication by `event_id` alone is `dropDuplicates(['event_id'])` WITH a watermark, which bounds state without making time part of the key. Option C is incidental. Option D is wrong — `dropDuplicates` accepts any number of columns.
questions.reference: Streaming deduplication: dropDuplicates with watermark for bounded state

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to add a CHECK constraint to a Delta table to enforce that the `age` column is always positive. Which SQL statement achieves this?

options.text: `ALTER TABLE users ADD CONSTRAINT age_positive CHECK (age > 0)`
`ALTER TABLE users SET TBLPROPERTIES ('constraint.age_positive' = 'age > 0')`
`CREATE INDEX age_check ON users (age) WHERE age > 0`
`ALTER TABLE users MODIFY COLUMN age INT NOT NULL CHECK (age > 0)`

questions.explanation: `ALTER TABLE users ADD CONSTRAINT age_positive CHECK (age > 0)` is the correct Delta Lake DDL syntax for adding a named CHECK constraint. Once added, any INSERT or UPDATE that would violate the constraint (i.e., insert a row with `age <= 0`) will fail with a constraint violation error. Existing rows are NOT retroactively validated when the constraint is added — only new writes are checked. Delta stores the constraint in the table metadata (transaction log). Option B (TBLPROPERTIES) is how CDF or other table properties are set — it is not the syntax for CHECK constraints. Option C (CREATE INDEX) is not a Delta Lake feature. Option D is not valid Delta DDL syntax.
questions.reference: Delta Lake table constraints: ALTER TABLE ADD CONSTRAINT CHECK

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates a Delta table with a generated column:

```sql
CREATE TABLE orders (
  order_id BIGINT,
  order_timestamp TIMESTAMP,
  order_date DATE GENERATED ALWAYS AS (CAST(order_timestamp AS DATE))
) PARTITIONED BY (order_date);
```

What is the PRIMARY benefit of using a generated column for `order_date`?

options.text: Generated columns are computed at query time, reducing storage space compared to storing the derived value
Delta Lake can automatically use the generated column for partition pruning even when the query filters on `order_timestamp`, because it can derive the partition from the expression
Generated columns prevent users from ever querying the `order_date` column directly, enforcing data abstraction
Generated columns allow the source system to omit `order_date` from writes, and Delta silently sets it to NULL

questions.explanation: Generated columns in Delta Lake are stored values derived from a deterministic expression over other columns — they are computed at write time and persisted, not computed at query time. The key optimization: when a generated column is a function of another column and the table is partitioned by the generated column, Delta Lake can infer partition values from filters on the source column. So `WHERE order_timestamp BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'` automatically enables partition pruning on `order_date` without the user explicitly filtering on `order_date`. This makes the partitioning nearly transparent to query writers. Option A is wrong — generated columns ARE stored, they don't save space. Option C is wrong — generated columns are queryable. Option D is wrong — generated columns cannot be explicitly set by writers; Delta enforces the expression.
questions.reference: Delta Lake generated columns: partition pruning via expression inference

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to decide between Liquid Clustering (`CLUSTER BY`) and Z-ORDER for a large Delta table that receives continuous streaming writes and is frequently queried by various ad hoc filter combinations. Which statement CORRECTLY compares the two approaches?

options.text: Liquid Clustering and Z-ORDER are functionally identical; the choice depends only on syntax preference
Liquid Clustering incrementally clusters new data without requiring a full OPTIMIZE rewrite; Z-ORDER requires running OPTIMIZE on the entire table to take effect on new files
Liquid Clustering requires a full table rewrite on every insert, making it unsuitable for streaming tables
Z-ORDER supports more clustering columns than Liquid Clustering, which is limited to one column

questions.explanation: Liquid Clustering (introduced with `CLUSTER BY` syntax) is designed to address the limitations of Z-ORDER for frequently written tables. Z-ORDER requires running `OPTIMIZE ZORDER BY (cols)` manually or on a schedule — new files written after the last OPTIMIZE are NOT ZORDERed and gain no data skipping benefit until the next OPTIMIZE. Liquid Clustering, by contrast, maintains cluster metadata and incrementally clusters new data over time without requiring a full table rewrite. This makes it much better suited to streaming tables with continuous ingestion. Both approaches improve data skipping; the key difference is the maintenance model. Option A is wrong — they have different operational models. Option C is wrong — Liquid Clustering is lightweight and streaming-friendly. Option D is wrong — Liquid Clustering supports multiple columns in `CLUSTER BY`.
questions.reference: Delta Lake Liquid Clustering vs Z-ORDER: incremental vs full-rewrite clustering

questions.stem: A data engineer enables Delta Lake column mapping on a table using `delta.columnMapping.mode = 'name'`. What capability does this unlock that is NOT available without column mapping?

options.text: The ability to use ZORDER on more than 4 columns simultaneously
The ability to DROP COLUMN from a Delta table without rewriting all data files
The ability to enable Change Data Feed on the table
The ability to partition the table by a column with more than 1,000 distinct values

questions.explanation: Column mapping (`delta.columnMapping.mode = 'name'`) decouples the logical column name from the physical Parquet field name stored in data files. Without column mapping, each column's logical name IS its physical name in the Parquet files — so renaming or dropping a column requires rewriting all data files. With column mapping enabled, logical names can be changed by updating only the transaction log metadata, without touching Parquet files. Critically, `DROP COLUMN` becomes a metadata-only operation — the Parquet files still contain the column data, but the transaction log stops exposing it to readers. This is a significant DDL enabler. Requires Delta protocol version 2 and Databricks Runtime 10.2+. Options A, C, and D are not features unlocked by column mapping.
questions.reference: Delta Lake column mapping: DROP COLUMN without file rewrite

questions.stem: A data engineering team creates a SHALLOW CLONE of a production table to use as a sandbox for a data science project. Why is SHALLOW CLONE commonly preferred over DEEP CLONE for this use case?

options.text: SHALLOW CLONE is the only clone type that preserves the table's partition structure
SHALLOW CLONE is fast and cheap because it copies only the transaction log metadata, not the data files — allowing experimentation without duplicating storage costs
SHALLOW CLONE creates a live mirror that automatically syncs with production changes
SHALLOW CLONE has a lower DBU cost per operation than DEEP CLONE

questions.explanation: SHALLOW CLONE copies only the Delta transaction log (metadata) to the destination location — the Parquet data files remain at the source location and are referenced in place. This makes SHALLOW CLONE near-instantaneous and storage-free regardless of table size (no data is copied). For sandbox/experimentation scenarios where the team wants to read the data and perhaps write a small amount of test output, SHALLOW CLONE is ideal. The key caveat is dependency on the source: if the source table's files are vacuum'd, the shallow clone breaks. Use DEEP CLONE when you need full independence. Option A is wrong — both clone types preserve partitioning. Option C is wrong — SHALLOW CLONE is a point-in-time snapshot, not a live sync. Option D is not how DBU costs work for clone operations.
questions.reference: Delta Lake SHALLOW CLONE: metadata-only copy for sandboxing

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to restore a Delta table to its state at version 15 after a bad MERGE accidentally corrupted data. Which statement CORRECTLY restores the table?

options.text: `SELECT * FROM my_table VERSION AS OF 15` followed by writing the result back to the table
`RESTORE TABLE my_table TO VERSION AS OF 15`
`VACUUM my_table RETAIN 0 HOURS` followed by reading version 15
`ALTER TABLE my_table ROLLBACK TO VERSION 15`

questions.explanation: `RESTORE TABLE my_table TO VERSION AS OF 15` is the correct Delta Lake DDL command to restore a table to a specific historical version. The command adds a new transaction log entry that points to the files corresponding to version 15, effectively making the table's current state identical to version 15 without deleting the transaction log history. You can also restore by timestamp: `RESTORE TABLE my_table TO TIMESTAMP AS OF '2024-01-15'`. The RESTORE operation is itself recorded in the transaction log, so the history of the corruption and the restore are both preserved. Option A requires an explicit read + overwrite which is more complex and loses atomicity. Option C is destructive and would break version 15 access. Option D is not valid Delta DDL syntax.
questions.reference: Delta Lake RESTORE TABLE: version-based restore

questions.stem: An organization wants to use a service principal (instead of a human user) to run automated Databricks jobs that read and write Unity Catalog tables. What must be done in Unity Catalog to grant the service principal access to a specific table?

options.text: Service principals cannot access Unity Catalog tables; only human users and groups can be granted privileges
Register the service principal in Unity Catalog's account console, then use standard GRANT statements with the service principal's application ID to assign privileges
Use an IAM role policy in the cloud console to grant the service principal access; Unity Catalog inherits cloud IAM policies automatically
Add the service principal to a Databricks admin group, which automatically grants all catalog privileges

questions.explanation: Service principals are first-class security principals in Unity Catalog alongside users and groups. The process: (1) Create the service principal in the Databricks account console (or sync from Azure AD/Okta), (2) Use standard `GRANT` statements with the service principal as the recipient: `GRANT USE CATALOG ON CATALOG prod TO <service_principal_id>; GRANT USE SCHEMA ON SCHEMA prod.sales TO <service_principal_id>; GRANT SELECT ON TABLE prod.sales.orders TO <service_principal_id>`. Service principals can be added to groups to simplify privilege management. Option A is wrong — service principals are fully supported. Option C is wrong — Unity Catalog has its own privilege model independent of cloud IAM (though storage credentials wrap cloud IAM for storage access). Option D is wrong — admin group membership grants workspace admin capabilities, not arbitrary catalog privileges.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog service principals: creating and granting privileges

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to query Unity Catalog audit logs to find all SELECT queries executed on the `prod.sales.orders` table in the last 7 days. Which system table contains this information?

options.text: `system.information_schema.table_privileges`
`system.access.audit`
`system.runtime.query_history`
`delta.audit_log` on the orders table

questions.explanation: The `system.access.audit` table in Unity Catalog's system schema captures all data access events, including SELECT queries, DML operations, DDL statements, and permission changes. Each row records: the timestamp, user/service principal, action type (e.g., `commandSubmit`, `tableAccess`), the object accessed (catalog/schema/table name), query text, and workspace/cluster information. To find SELECT queries on `prod.sales.orders`: `SELECT * FROM system.access.audit WHERE event_time > CURRENT_TIMESTAMP() - INTERVAL 7 DAYS AND request_params.table_full_name = 'prod.sales.orders' AND action_name = 'commandSubmit'`. Option A (`table_privileges`) shows granted privileges, not access events. Option C (`system.runtime.query_history`) shows query execution history but is not the audit log. Option D is not a valid table reference.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog system tables: system.access.audit for data access auditing

questions.stem:
A data engineer adds a tag to a Delta table in Unity Catalog:

```sql
ALTER TABLE prod.sales.orders SET TAGS ('pii' = 'true', 'domain' = 'finance');
```

How can tags be used to manage data governance at scale?

options.text: Tags automatically encrypt columns labeled with 'pii' = 'true'
Tags are metadata annotations that can be used to discover, classify, and apply governance policies to data assets at scale without modifying the tables themselves
Tags replace the need for GRANT/REVOKE statements by automatically managing access based on tag values
Tags trigger automatic VACUUM operations on tables labeled 'pii' to remove historical versions of sensitive data

questions.explanation: Tags in Unity Catalog are key-value metadata annotations on catalogs, schemas, tables, views, and columns. They serve as a governance and discovery layer: (1) Data discovery — search for all tables with `domain = 'finance'` across your entire metastore using `SEARCH` or by querying `information_schema.table_tags`. (2) Policy application — identify PII tables programmatically and apply masking policies, audit requirements, or retention rules based on tags. (3) Cost attribution — tag tables by business unit to track data usage. Tags do NOT automatically enforce any security or technical behavior — they are metadata. Security controls still require explicit GRANT/REVOKE or masking policies. Option A is wrong — tags don't auto-encrypt. Option C is wrong — tags don't replace privilege management. Option D is wrong — tags don't trigger VACUUM.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog tags: ALTER TABLE SET TAGS, metadata-driven governance

questions.stem: A data company wants to share a curated Delta table with an external partner organization that uses a different Databricks workspace (or no Databricks at all). Which Unity Catalog feature enables this WITHOUT giving the partner direct credentials to the underlying cloud storage?

options.text: Create an External Location and share the S3 path with the partner
Use Delta Sharing: create a Share object containing the table, create a Recipient for the partner, and grant SELECT on the table to the Share
Enable CDF on the table and provide the partner with the CDF read token
DEEP CLONE the table to a partner-accessible S3 bucket and email the AWS credentials

questions.explanation: Delta Sharing is an open protocol for secure cross-organization data sharing. The workflow: (1) `CREATE SHARE partner_share` — creates a named share object, (2) `ALTER SHARE partner_share ADD TABLE prod.sales.summary` — adds the table to the share, (3) `CREATE RECIPIENT partner_org` — creates a recipient identity for the external organization, (4) `GRANT SELECT ON TABLE prod.sales.summary TO SHARE partner_share` — grants the share access to the table, (5) `GRANT SELECT ON SHARE partner_share TO RECIPIENT partner_org` — grants the recipient access to the share. The partner receives a sharing token and reads data via the Delta Sharing protocol — they never need cloud storage credentials or access to the underlying S3/ADLS bucket. Option A exposes raw cloud storage credentials. Option C is not a real sharing mechanism. Option D is insecure and not governed.
questions.reference: Delta Sharing: CREATE SHARE, RECIPIENT, cross-organization data sharing

questions.stem: In Unity Catalog, privilege inheritance flows down the object hierarchy. Which statement CORRECTLY describes how GRANT on a catalog propagates?

options.text: Granting SELECT on a catalog automatically grants SELECT on all schemas, tables, and views within that catalog
Granting USE CATALOG on a catalog allows a principal to list schemas within the catalog and navigate into them, but does NOT automatically grant SELECT on any tables
All privileges granted on a catalog are automatically inherited by all securable objects within the catalog, including SELECT, CREATE TABLE, and MODIFY
Unity Catalog does not support catalog-level GRANT; privileges must be granted at the schema or table level

questions.explanation: Unity Catalog has a nuanced inheritance model. `USE CATALOG` is a navigational privilege — it allows a principal to see that the catalog exists and to enter it, but does NOT grant access to any data within. To actually read a table, a principal also needs `USE SCHEMA` on the containing schema AND `SELECT` on the specific table (or schema, for all tables in the schema). No privilege grants automatic access to all child objects: `SELECT ON CATALOG` does NOT exist as a privilege that propagates down. Each level requires its own explicit grant. Option A is wrong — SELECT on a catalog doesn't flow to tables. Option C is wrong — inheritance is limited to navigational privileges (`USE CATALOG` on a catalog doesn't cascade to `SELECT` on tables). Option D is wrong — catalog-level grants ARE supported for `USE CATALOG`, `CREATE SCHEMA`, etc.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog privilege inheritance: USE CATALOG vs SELECT propagation

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to track Structured Streaming metrics programmatically in real time — specifically the input rate, processing rate, and trigger execution time for each micro-batch. Which Spark API enables this?

options.text: Implement a `StreamingQueryListener` and register it with `spark.streams.addListener()` to receive `onQueryProgress` events with detailed metrics per micro-batch
Query `spark.streams.active` after each micro-batch to get the current streaming statistics
Enable `spark.sql.streaming.metricsEnabled = true` and read from the Ganglia metrics UI
Use `df.explain()` on the streaming DataFrame to expose per-batch performance statistics

questions.explanation: `StreamingQueryListener` is the programmatic API for monitoring Structured Streaming jobs. You implement the listener interface with three methods: `onQueryStarted`, `onQueryProgress`, and `onQueryTerminated`. The `onQueryProgress` callback receives a `StreamingQueryProgress` object on every micro-batch completion, containing: `inputRowsPerSecond`, `processedRowsPerSecond`, `triggerExecution.latencyMs`, `stateOperators` (state size metrics), `sources` (per-source offset ranges), and more. Register it with `spark.streams.addListener(myListener)`. This enables custom alerting, metric forwarding to monitoring systems (e.g., Datadog, Prometheus), or logging to Delta tables. Option B gives only a snapshot of current active queries, not per-batch history. Option C is partially true but Ganglia is coarse-grained and not Structured Streaming-specific. Option D explains the plan, not runtime metrics.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming StreamingQueryListener: per-batch metrics monitoring

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `EXPLAIN` on a SQL query that filters on a Delta table column and wants to verify that Delta's predicate pushdown is working (i.e., the filter is being applied during file scanning, not after reading all files). What should they look for in the EXPLAIN output?

options.text: A `Filter` node appearing ABOVE the `Scan` node in the physical plan, indicating the filter runs after the scan
A `PushedFilters` entry within the `Scan` node in the physical plan, showing the filter predicates are evaluated during the scan
A `BroadcastExchange` node replacing the `Filter` node when pushdown is active
The absence of any `Filter` node in the physical plan

questions.explanation: In Spark's physical plan (visible via `EXPLAIN` or the Spark UI SQL tab), the `Scan` node for a Delta table shows a `PushedFilters` field listing any filter predicates that have been pushed down to the scan layer. When predicate pushdown is active, the filter is evaluated during file reading (Parquet column/row group pruning), reducing the amount of data deserialized. If pushdown is NOT working (e.g., the column has no statistics or uses an unsupported expression), the scan reads all data and a separate `Filter` node above the scan applies the predicate post-read. To confirm pushdown: look for `PushedFilters: [IsNotNull(col), EqualTo(col,value)]` inside the `FileScan` node. Option A describes the fallback case where pushdown did NOT occur. Option C is unrelated. Option D is wrong — filters still appear, just embedded in the scan node.
questions.reference: Spark EXPLAIN: PushedFilters in Scan node for predicate pushdown verification

questions.stem:
A data engineer queries Delta table history using:

```sql
DESCRIBE HISTORY prod.sales.transactions LIMIT 5;
```

Which information is NOT contained in the output of `DESCRIBE HISTORY`?

options.text: The version number of each commit
The username or service principal that performed the operation
The `operationParameters` showing details like merge conditions or partition overwrite mode used
The physical Parquet file paths that were added or removed in each version

questions.explanation: `DESCRIBE HISTORY` shows metadata about each transaction in the Delta transaction log. It includes: `version` (commit number), `timestamp` (when the commit occurred), `userId`, `userName` (who performed the operation), `operation` (e.g., WRITE, MERGE, OPTIMIZE, DELETE), `operationParameters` (e.g., the MERGE predicate, partition values, output mode), `operationMetrics` (e.g., numFilesAdded, numRowsUpdated), and `userMetadata` if set. What it does NOT show is the physical file paths added/removed. To see which specific Parquet files were part of a commit, you must read the raw transaction log JSON files in `_delta_log/` or use `spark.read.format('delta').option('versionAsOf', n).load(path)` and compare. Options A, B, and C are all present in DESCRIBE HISTORY output.
questions.reference: DESCRIBE HISTORY: Delta transaction log metadata, operationParameters

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `ANALYZE TABLE prod.sales.orders COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS` before a complex multi-table JOIN query. What does this enable that was not possible before?

options.text: The statistics enable Databricks' cost-based optimizer (CBO) to choose better join strategies (e.g., preferring broadcast over sort-merge for smaller tables) and join ordering for multi-table joins
The statistics are used by VACUUM to decide which files to delete based on data freshness
The statistics automatically repartition the table to match the number of distinct values per column
The statistics replace the need for ZORDER by providing exact column-to-file mappings

questions.explanation: `ANALYZE TABLE ... COMPUTE STATISTICS` collects extended column-level statistics: null count, number of distinct values (NDV), min, max, and average column size. These statistics feed Databricks' cost-based optimizer (CBO), which uses them to: (1) Estimate the cardinality and selectivity of joins and filters, (2) Choose optimal join strategies (a table with estimated 1M rows is very different from one with 100 rows — the smaller one should be broadcast), (3) Reorder multi-table joins to minimize intermediate data sizes. Without CBO statistics, Spark makes guesses based on file sizes (coarser estimates). Delta Lake collects min/max per file for data skipping, but `ANALYZE TABLE` provides table-wide statistics for plan optimization. Option B is wrong — VACUUM uses file timestamps, not statistics. Option C is wrong — statistics don't auto-repartition. Option D is wrong — ZORDER uses statistics, but CBO stats serve a different purpose.
questions.reference: ANALYZE TABLE COMPUTE STATISTICS: cost-based optimizer (CBO) for join optimization

questions.stem:
A data engineer sets the following table property:

```sql
ALTER TABLE events SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.targetFileSize' = '134217728');
```

What does this property control and what is the value in MB?

options.text: It sets the maximum allowed file size before a write is split; 134217728 bytes = 128 MB
It sets the target output file size for OPTIMIZE operations; 134217728 bytes = 128 MB, overriding the default ~1GB target
It sets the Parquet row group size; 134217728 bytes = 128 MB per row group
It sets the maximum Delta log entry size; entries exceeding 128 MB trigger a checkpoint
